---
title: "Facilitator guide – Applied Replication for Data Skills"
subtitle: "Open Research Conference, Newcastle University · 16 June 2026 · 10:15–12:45 · Room 1.06 Henry Daysh"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: false
---

::: {.callout-note}
**This document is for the facilitator's eyes only.** It is rendered as part of the site but not included in the navbar.
:::

---

## The night before (15 June)

- [ ] Render the full site: `quarto render` from the workspace root; check no errors; push; confirm Pages build completes on GitHub Actions.
- [ ] Test the full participant loop from a throwaway GitHub account: "Use this template" → own public repo → enable Pages → clone in Positron → `quarto preview` renders skeleton → push → Action deploys → live URL appears.
- [ ] Open the two pinned Discussion threads in browser tabs and verify they are visible to a logged-out visitor: "Results board & gallery" and "Help / I'm stuck".
- [ ] Charge laptop. Confirm charger is in bag.
- [ ] Print cheatsheets (one per pair = ~10 copies; file: `exercise/cheatsheet.qmd` rendered PDF or A4 HTML printout).
- [ ] Prepare the Vevox session at `[VEVOX-LINK]`; confirm the three poll moments are queued; test that the QR on the slide resolves correctly. Note the fallback: all polls also work as hands-up. Nothing downstream depends on Vevox output.
- [ ] Cut ~20 sticky notes: green (= "working") and red/orange (= "help needed") per pair table.
- [ ] Set `_variables.yml` date/room fields if not already finalised. Confirm the OSF fork `6zqct` is still public (anonymous `osfr` download works without login; cross-check that `osf.io/8rtwe` also remains public as the archival record).
- [ ] Fix the fork wiki self-link: the fork's wiki currently says "https://osf.io/zb3ec (this project)" – zb3ec is a deleted earlier fork (HTTP 410). Update the wiki to point to the correct self-link: https://osf.io/6zqct/.

---

## Room setup (09:45–10:15)

- [ ] Project the slide deck (`slides/index.qmd`) on the room screen from 09:45.
- [ ] Show **two QR codes simultaneously** on the opening title slide (or on a holding slide before the deck):
  - Site: `https://codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/`
  - Template repo: `https://github.com/CodeMoreh/replication-lab`
- [ ] Write on the whiteboard (or have a slide):
  - Room wifi SSID and password
  - Short URL to the site
  - The five in-room URLs from the cheatsheet
- [ ] Check power strips are accessible at tables; room has ~20 seats.
- [ ] Place printed cheatsheets on tables (one per pair position).
- [ ] Place sticky note pairs (green + red/orange) on each table.
- [ ] If a second screen/laptop is available, log into GitHub Discussions gallery tab – useful to watch result lines land live during the debrief.
- [ ] Confirm room wifi allows outbound HTTPS to `github.com`, `osf.io`, `codemoreh.github.io`. If not, activate instructor hotspot before the session starts.

---

## Run sheet

| Clock | Segment | What I do | What participants do | Watch out for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10:15 | **Welcome & pairs** (5′) | Welcome. State premise in two sentences: "In April *Nature* published three papers on trust in social science. I was inside one of them – today you sit where I sat." Show roadmap slide. Ask: "Who ran the check script and got all green?" – those hands spread out around the room, one per pair (see pair formation script below). Display wifi + QR codes. | Find a partner, scan QR, note the site URL. | Keep this brisk – 5 min hard cap. If check-script hands are fewer than ~5, proceed anyway; Track A absorbs. |
| 10:20 | **Talk I – the three Rs** (18′) | Walk slides 1–14 at measured pace. Reveal the three *Nature* papers in sequence. Use the quiz fragments on slide 5 ("I rerun your Stata do-file on your data – which R?") as hands-up beats. Land on the punchline: reproducible ≠ robust ≠ replicable, the three indicators are near-uncorrelated. | Listen; hands-up quiz beats; brief pair chat on slide 5 prompt. | Don't over-run. If behind after Miske (slide 8), cut Tyner to one sentence ("55% replicated; effect sizes halved") and skip moderators slide. |
| 10:38 | **Talk II – the Teney case** (10′) | Slide 15: Teney (2016) in 60 seconds. Slide 16: the claim card verbatim. Slide 17: SCORE's "not reproduced" – pause, let it land. Slides 18–19: reveal the five-analyst plot **one row at a time**; ask after each: "Same data. Same claim. Why five answers?" End: "Today you are analyst no. 6." | Watch the reveal; absorb the range; begin to wonder what *their* choice would be. | The five-analyst table is the emotional hook. Don't rush slide 18. |
| 10:48 | **Setup sprint – two tracks** (15′) | Announce Track A (browser) vs Track B (full pipeline). Read out the pair formation result: Track B pairs drive the template flow; Track A pairs open the browser-lab page directly. Project the setup page URL. Circulate with sticky notes: green = working, red/orange = help. Watch the room; triage red notes first. At 11:00 call time: "If you're not rendering yet, open the browser-lab page now – you can do everything there." | Track B: "Use this template" → own public repo → enable Pages → clone in Positron → `quarto preview`. Track A: navigate to site → Browser Lab page → wait for webR engine (~10–30 s). | The setup sprint is the highest-risk segment. Have the github.dev fallback in mind. Announce it at 10:55 if red notes persist: "Press `.` on your repo on GitHub – that's enough to edit and commit." |
| 11:03 | **Concepts – estimand + DAG** (18′) | Mini-lecture 8′ (slides 20–26): Silberzahn red-card study → A&B's diagnosis (four estimands, dispersion shrinks sevenfold when one is fixed) → Lundberg's three steps → confounder/mediator/collider crash-course → the Teney DAG, half-built. Then: "Now you finish it. Open Task C in your report." Guide 10′ hands-on. At ~11:20 ask one pair to share their adjustment-set output. | Write the estimand sentence; complete the DAG edges they believe in; run `adjustmentSets()`; note what two-way FE adjusts for and what it cannot. | If behind by >5 min at 11:03, compress to demo-only: run `adjustmentSets()` live on the projector, participants note the output, skip the pair build step. |
| 11:21 | **Reproduce the constrained result** (12′) | Announce Task B + D1. "Run `R/get_data.R` – you are literally fetching from the published OSF project." Then fit the two-way FE model. Target: t ≈ −3.80. Circulate; watch for data-fetch failures (fallback chain is built-in). After ~8 min: "Does anyone have t = −3.80?" – show of hands; celebrate it. Mention: "`feols` and `plm` give the same point estimate; the SE difference is itself the lesson." | Run `get_data.R`; explore the panel; fit the constrained model; confirm t ≈ −3.80; first commit + push. | OSF rate-limiting is unlikely (28 KB file) but the fallback chain handles it silently. If no one gets the target t, check they used `unemp_c` (centred), not raw `unemp`. |
| 11:33 | **Break** (10′) | "Ten-minute break – back at 11:43." Monitor the Discussion help thread during the break; answer any posted questions. | Break; post questions to Discussion if stuck. | Keep to 10 min. |
| 11:43 | **Your analytical path** (22′) | Announce Task D2. "Pick ONE deviation from the spec menu. Before you run it – preregister it: Track B commit message `prereg: …` before running; Track A post your intention to the Discussion thread first." Circulate; prompt pairs who are blank: "What does your DAG say about GDP growth – confounder or co-exposure?" After ~15 min: "Run your spec, then post the `report_result()` line to the Results board thread." | Choose one spec; justify it in one sentence grounded in the DAG; preregister; run; post result to Discussion thread. | Watch for pairs who preregister after running – gently call it out as a teaching point, not a reprimand. Fast pairs: point to Task F (second spec or Hausman test). |
| 12:05 | **Share & publish** (10′) | "Now publish. Track B: commit, push – Action deploys in ~1–2 min. Track A: we'll do a 'first repo in 3 minutes' live demo – watch." Demo the web-upload flow on the projector: navigate to `github.com/new`, upload script/qmd, repo created. Remind: paste the repo URL (+ Pages URL if deployed) into the Results board thread. | Track B: final commit + push; watch Action; paste URLs. Track A: follow the demo; upload files; paste repo URL. | Actions queue can be slow on conference wifi. Reassure: "The Action will finish even after we leave – the URL will work." |
| 12:15 | **Multiverse debrief** (12′) | Show the pre-computed spec curve (from `data/spec_grid.csv`) on the slides. Then overlay the class results posted to the thread. Ask questions from the debrief bank below. Land on: "We just did a mini-Multi100. Which menu choices moved the estimate most? Is the claim robust 'enough'?" | Look at the chart; locate their own result; contribute to discussion. | If fewer than 4 result lines in the thread, use the pre-computed grid alone – it's still a rich chart. Never type credentials on the projector. |
| 12:27 | **Wrap** (5′) | Recap the three skills: reproducible data retrieval + version-controlled literate report + published multiverse contribution. "You now own a public repo and a live site – that's a portfolio piece." Show take-home resources slide. Share Vevox feedback QR (`[VEVOX-LINK]`). Thank everyone. | Optionally fill in feedback poll; note URLs to keep. | If Vevox fails, ask for one-word verbal feedback: "What's the one thing you'll take away?" |

---

## Timing flex notes

### If 5 minutes behind

- Cut the **Miske moderators** content (slide 9): state one line ("data-sharing policies predict availability") and move on.
- Tighten the estimand sentence-writing to a show-of-hands: "Who wrote 'country-year' as the unit? Who wrote 'individual'?" – makes the point without pair work.

### If 10 minutes behind

- Above, plus: compress the DAG guided exercise to live demo only – run `adjustmentSets()` on the projector, participants note the answer, no individual build.
- Drop the Track B "first commit + push" call during the reproduce segment (participants still commit later; Action still deploys).

### If 15 minutes behind

- All of the above, plus: drop the **second-spec / Task F** stretch entirely (already optional).
- Cut the publish demo to a single sentence and a QR to the setup page's "First repo" instructions.
- If still behind: the multiverse debrief shrinks to 7 minutes (show the chart, ask two questions from the bank, wrap). The debrief is the intellectual payoff – protect at least 7 minutes for it.

**Compressible in order:** Miske moderators → DAG hands-on → second commit during reproduce → publish demo → debrief (to 7 min minimum).
**Never cut:** the five-analyst reveal; the preregister moment; posting to the Results board thread.

---

## Contingency table

| Situation | What to do |
|---|---|
| **Participants arrive without GitHub account or installs** | Pair them immediately with a Track B partner. Direct to Track A (browser lab) as their primary track – they can do Tasks A–E entirely in the browser. No setup required. |
| **Track A (browser lab) webR fails to load** | Rare; try a hard reload. If it persists, direct the pair to `github.dev` (see below) for editing; they can run code in Positron on their partner's machine. As last resort: follow along on the projector. |
| **Local git or Positron broken** | Direct to `github.dev`: press `.` on the repo page in a browser. Full edit + commit + push interface; the Action still deploys. Code execution is lost, but Tasks A, C (written), E all work. |
| **Room wifi blocks or throttles GitHub/OSF** | Data fallback chain (osfr → direct osf.io URL → site copy at `codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/data/rep_data.csv`) handles silently for most pairs. If GitHub itself is blocked, activate the **instructor hotspot** (SSID/password on a slip of paper, not on the projector). |
| **OSF rate-limits simultaneous downloads** | File is 28 KB – rate-limiting is very unlikely. The fallback chain's third step (site copy) will serve. Announce: "If the OSF download hangs, the script tries a backup URL automatically – just wait 10 seconds." |
| **GitHub Actions queue very slow** | Publishing is async by design; nothing in the session blocks on it. Reassure participants the URL will work within minutes. The gallery thread accepts repo URLs too, not only Pages URLs. |
| **Positron GitHub OAuth fails (managed laptop)** | Cheatsheet includes the Git Credential Manager browser-auth path. If that also fails: `git remote set-url origin https://TOKEN@github.com/...` with a classic PAT (participant creates one in GitHub Settings → Developer settings). As absolute last resort: one shared demo account – **project the screen, never type the credentials on the projector**. |
| **Vevox fails** | All poll moments have been designed to work as hands-up. Nothing downstream depends on poll output. Proceed without Vevox. |
| **Pair migration needed (one laptop entirely broken)** | Both participants join a working pair as a trio. Three people on one laptop is fine – more conversation, not less. |

---

## Pair formation script

**Goal:** match prepared (Track B) laptops with unprepared or browser-only laptops in under 3 minutes.

> "Before we start – quick show of hands: who ran the check script this morning and got all green ticks? Keep your hand up."

Count. Expect roughly half the room (some will have done it, some won't).

> "Great. Those of you with hands up – you're the Track B drivers. Please spread out around the room, one per table or one per pair of seats. Leave a space next to you."

Wait 30 seconds for movement.

> "Everyone else – sit next to one of the Track B folks. You're the Track A partner, which means you'll be running everything in the browser. You'll do all the same tasks; your partner will show you the full pipeline alongside."

If Track B volunteers are fewer than ~5:

> "No problem – we'll mostly work in the browser today. Every table, find a partner. Both of you open the browser lab page now."

If everyone has a green check script:

> "Excellent. Pair up with the person next to you. Both of you have the full pipeline – one of you will also keep the browser-lab page open as a backup."

**Sticky-note protocol:** hand each pair one green note (place visible when working fine) and one red/orange note (display when stuck). Circulate and respond to red notes during the setup sprint and exercise segments.

---

## Debrief question bank (multiverse discussion, 12:15–12:27)

Use 4–6 of these, reading the room for which have the richest answers given what pairs posted.

1. **Sign flip:** "Did anyone's specification flip the sign – unemployment associated with *more* positive framing? If not, what does that tell us about the claim's robustness?"
2. **Significance loss:** "Did any specification lose statistical significance? What was the spec? Is that a reason to doubt the claim, or a reason to scrutinise that particular spec?"
3. **Axis that moved estimates most:** "Looking at the chart – which menu axis spread estimates furthest apart? Was it the outcome, the sample, the estimator? What does that say about where the 'forking path' is most consequential?"
4. **Justification quality:** "Who found their DAG helpful in justifying their choice? Who felt the DAG didn't settle the question – that it could justify multiple specs equally well?"
5. **Preregistration value:** "Track B partners – did writing `prereg:` in your commit message before running change how you felt about the result? Would you have made a different choice if you'd run it first?"
6. **Robustness tolerance:** "The Multi100 paper uses two tolerance bands: ±0.05 d and ±0.20 d. Looking at our class results – is the Teney claim robust by the tight standard? The loose standard? What tolerance is appropriate for a policy-relevant claim about unemployment and EU attitudes?"
7. **Against the five analysts:** "Our class results overlap with the five Multi100 analysts' range. Does that mean we replicated the Multi100 multiverse? What would it mean if we'd found a result *outside* their range?"
8. **Take-home:** "Auspurg and Brüderl found that fixing the estimand shrank dispersion sevenfold. Did we test that today? Would a clearer estimand have narrowed our class multiverse?"
9. **The three Rs on one paper:** "The same Teney paper failed push-button reproduction (despite author-provided code), survived robustness in sign but not in magnitude, and returned a sign flip in a preregistered replication. Which of the three Rs should worry us most here, and why? Does the answer depend on what you want the finding to be used for?"

---

## After the session

- [ ] Paste all result lines from the Discussion thread into `results.qmd` (the class-results section); commit and push – the page updates automatically.
- [ ] Post a thank-you reply in the "Results board & gallery" Discussion thread: acknowledge contributions, link to the updated results page, note when the full multiverse chart will be live.
- [ ] Post a brief reply in the "Help / I'm stuck" thread: confirm the session is over, note that questions are still welcome, link to resources.
- [ ] Note any timing issues, contingency events, or participant feedback for the improvement log. Key questions to record:
  - Which segment ran longest?
  - Did the browser-lab webR load reliably on room wifi?
  - How many pairs reached the publish step?
  - Any `report_result()` formatting errors that broke the results board?
- [ ] Archive the Vevox results (export PDF or screenshot before the session expires).
